CRC Liquid-Biopsy Pipeline — Results Summary

Tutorial deliverable for Indigo. Two independent classifiers for colorectal cancer (CRC), both trained on real public data, with cross-validated performance and biological feature interpretation.

Headline numbers

ModalitySourcen samplesBest model10-fold CV AUC
Stool microbiomecuratedMetagenomicData (7 cohorts)648 (314 CRC, 334 control)RandomForest0.818
Plasma cfDNA fragment lengthsFinaleDB (48 CRC + 80 Cristiano-2019 healthy)128Logistic regression0.972 (all studies)
0.951 (Cristiano-only)
Bottom line: Both signals are real and recover textbook biology. The microbiome AUC of 0.818 is consistent with the Thomas et al. 2019 Nature Medicine meta-analysis (avg 0.84). The cfDNA mean-length shift (155.6 bp CRC vs 167.4 bp healthy) is the canonical 12-bp shortening that Cristiano et al. 2019 Nature reported.

Part A — Microbiome classifier

Data

649 stool samples drawn from 7 published CRC cohorts (ZellerG_2014, FengQ_2015, YuJ_2015, VogtmannE_2016, HanniganGD_2017, ThomasAM_2018a, ThomasAM_2018b) via the curatedMetagenomicData ExperimentHub release. Restricted to CRC vs control (adenomas dropped) for binary classification: 314 CRC + 334 controls = 648 samples × 4,771 species.

Model

RandomForest (500 trees, min_samples_leaf=5, balanced class weight) on log10-transformed relative abundances. 10-fold stratified cross-validation; every sample predicted by a model that never saw it during training.

Top features (verified biology)

Top 10 species by Gini importance:

RankSpeciesDirectionImportance
1Peptostreptococcus stomatisHigher in CRC0.0277
2Parvimonas unclassifiedHigher in CRC0.0203
3Gemella morbillorumHigher in CRC0.0189
4Fusobacterium nucleatumHigher in CRC0.0172
5Parvimonas micraHigher in CRC0.0143
6Clostridium symbiosumHigher in CRC0.0142
7Solobacterium mooreiHigher in CRC0.0137
8Porphyromonas asaccharolyticaHigher in CRC0.0129
9Streptococcus salivariusHigher in control0.0107
10Bifidobacterium catenulatumHigher in control0.0101

Further down the list, protective butyrate producers Roseburia intestinalis (rank 16) and Faecalibacterium prausnitzii (rank 17) appear as control-enriched — also consistent with literature.

Part B — cfDNA fragment-length classifier

Data

Fragment-length histograms (Picard CollectInsertSizeMetrics output) downloaded from FinaleDB. 48 CRC plasma samples (Cristiano 2019 + Sun 2019 + Snyder 2016) and 80 randomly chosen Cristiano-2019 healthy plasma samples. Analysis restricted to 80–400 bp to avoid FinaleDB's 50-bp-trim short-fragment artifact.

Per-group fragment statistics

GroupnMean length (bp)Median (bp)Short/Long ratio
Healthy80167.4167.60.22
Colorectal cancer48155.6156.70.66

Classifiers

Single-feature AUCs (raw value, no model):

FeatureAUCDirection in CRC
mean_length0.978lower
median_length0.976lower
short_fraction (100–150 bp)0.960higher
short_long_ratio0.958higher
long_fraction (151–220 bp)0.951lower
mode_length0.853lower
mononuc_fraction (155–180 bp)0.734lower
sd_length0.682higher

A single number — mean fragment length — captures essentially all the signal. The full models barely improve on it.

Batch-effect check

What this means for the tutorial

  1. Real data is messy but consistent. Per-cohort microbiome AUCs ranged from 0.61 (HanniganGD_2017) to 0.89 (FengQ_2015). Pooled meta-analysis recovers the consensus signal that no single study could.
  2. Simple models often win. Logistic regression on 8 hand-picked features matched RandomForest on cfDNA. The cfDNA biology lives in one number — mean fragment length — and any more complex model risks overfitting to it.
  3. Always sanity-check features against domain knowledge. Both classifiers' top features are species and statistics that experts already recognize. If the top features had been unrecognized, that would be a flag to look for confounds.
  4. Check for batch effects. The all-studies cfDNA AUC of 0.972 dropped to 0.951 within Cristiano alone — still excellent, but quantifies how much of the headline number was per-study systematics.

Files delivered

All files are at /media/chew/Elements1/Chew/Rion/CRC_liquid_biopsy_Indigo/:

  • CRC_RealData_Indigo.ipynb — end-to-end student notebook (33 cells, runs top-to-bottom)
  • prepare_microbiome_data.R — regenerates the microbiome CSVs from public Bioconductor mirrors using base R + Biobase only (no curatedMetagenomicData install needed)
  • crc_species_abundance.csv, crc_sample_metadata.csv — microbiome data the notebook reads (8.5 MB + 46 KB)
  • 6 figure PNGs (the ones embedded above) and 5 supporting CSVs (per-sample predictions, full feature-importance ranking, FinaleDB sample manifest, cfDNA per-sample features)

Pipeline reproducibility: every number in this report comes from data downloaded fresh from public sources (mghp.osn.xsede.org Bioconductor mirror for microbiome, finaledb.research.cchmc.org S3 bucket for cfDNA). No synthetic data anywhere.
